petitioner_background: The petitioner is an art restorer from Russia who studied art restoration in Russia and earned certification as an art restorer qualified to conserve and restore artworks from the 16th century and later. She worked at an institution in Russia as leader of the restoration department from 1981 to 2006 and as deputy director for scientific research from 2006 to 2020. She entered the United States as a B-2 nonimmigrant visitor in May 2022 and seeks classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A. She claimed to have satisfied eight of the ten regulatory criteria.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of lesser nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards. Specific awards are not detailed in the AAO decision, but this criterion was addressed by the Director.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded that the Petitioner met this criterion relating to prizes or awards. The AAO did not disturb this finding and accepted that this criterion was satisfied.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner documented her membership on two 'Expert Councils' of the Ministry of Culture of the Republic of Karelia. In response to the RFE, she submitted meeting minutes (dating from 2016-2020) showing she participated in 'Consideration of candidates for awards from the Government of the Republic of Karelia to members of creative unions... and personal awards... for gifted students and graduate students.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director had asserted that 'the evidence does not show the petitioner actually reviewed any work or otherwise acted as a judge of the work of others' and dismissed the RFE evidence claiming it must have existed as of the date the petition was filed. The AAO rejected this reasoning, noting the meeting minutes all date from 2016-2020, several years before the May 2023 filing date. The AAO also clarified that while eligibility must be established at the time of filing, evidence that pre-dates the filing date but was submitted later is still valid. The AAO concluded the Petitioner established by a preponderance of the evidence that she meets this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted six research articles she authored. Some of the published works appear to be from academic conferences, at least some of which were national rather than local or regional.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director acknowledged only one of the Petitioner's articles and determined it did not qualify as scholarly because 'it appears that the intended audience may be the general public.' The AAO agreed with the Petitioner that the Director 'erred by disregarding the other five (5) research articles authored by the Petitioner.' The AAO noted that per USCIS Policy Manual F.2(B)(1), scholarly articles include published conference presentations at nationally or internationally recognized conferences. The Director did not address these materials or explain why they do not satisfy the requirements, so the AAO withdrew this determination.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that she restored numerous artworks that went on to be displayed. Her initial filing included evidence about a 2005 exhibition with a title variously translated, and a 2005 article from the Russian online publication Museums of Russia described this exhibition as being 'dedicated to the work of restorers,' specifically the Petitioner and a colleague. The record indicates that this exhibition focused on examples of restoration work, rather than paintings themselves.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded: 'The evidence does not establish that she created or developed the paintings because the petitioner restored the artwork and did not create them.' The AAO agreed with the general principle that when a painting is displayed, it is the work of the artist on display. However, the AAO found an important distinction in the 2005 exhibition, which was specifically dedicated to the work of restorers rather than the paintings themselves. The AAO concluded this 2005 exhibition appears sufficient to establish the display of the Petitioner's work at an artistic exhibition.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO withdrew the Director's decision and remanded the matter for entry of a new decision. The AAO concluded the Petitioner satisfied at least three of the regulatory criteria (judging, scholarly articles, and exhibition, in addition to awards which the Director had already granted), and therefore reserved discussion of the other claimed criteria. The matter was remanded for the Director to make a final merits determination, with particular attention to whether the Petitioner achieved national or international (rather than merely regional) acclaim, noting that the Republic of Karelia is a political subdivision of the Russian Federation rather than an independent nation, so recognition there is not necessarily national or international acclaim. The AAO also remanded because the Director did not adequately explain the basis for concluding the Petitioner had not shown she would continue working in her area of expertise or that her entry would substantially benefit the United States, particularly given her submitted plans to restore icons for Orthodox churches, consult on conservation/restoration/authentication of tempera paintings, and create a college-level course on art restoration.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537, 537 n.2 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO identified procedural errors by the Director: (1) the Director incorrectly asserted that qualifying evidence must have existed in the record at the time of filing, when in fact evidence originating before the filing date but submitted later (such as in response to an RFE) is valid; (2) the Director failed to address five of six scholarly articles submitted by the Petitioner without explanation; (3) the Director did not adequately explain the basis for concluding the Petitioner would not continue working in her area of expertise or that her entry would not substantially benefit the United States, violating the requirement under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(i) that the Director explain in writing the specific reasons for denial.
